specification.topic-statement: The tool registry maintains an ordered, overrideable in-memory set of tool definitions available to agents.

scope.in-scope: Storing, looking up, adding, replacing, clearing, sorting, and enumerating tool definitions (both external and internal). Growth strategy of the backing store.
scope.boundary: Tools are added to the registry by an external discovery process. How tools are found or how their schemas are obtained is outside this topic's scope.
This topic produces a JSON array of tool schemas for LLM consumption but does not interact with the LLM or serialize requests directly.

field.name: name
field.note: registry key; derived from executable filename

field.name: path
field.note: full filesystem path to the executable

field.name: schema
field.note: the tool's schema as returned by --schema

field.name: handler
field.note: invoked directly when the tool is called

field.name: completion hook
field.note: invoked after tool result is processed; null if not needed

behavior.creation: A new registry is created with an initial capacity of 16 slots and zero entries. Both the registry structure and its backing array are zero-initialized.
behavior.lookup:
Looking up a tool by name performs a linear scan from the first entry to the last. The first entry whose name matches exactly (case-sensitive) is returned. If no entry matches, nothing is returned.
Lookup is O(n) in the number of registered tools. There is no hash index or binary search despite the registry being kept sorted.
behavior.adding-an-external-tool:
When an external tool is added, the registry first looks up whether a tool with the same name already exists.
If an entry with the same name already exists, it is replaced in-place. The old name string, path string, and schema document are freed. New copies of the name and path strings are allocated under the registry. The new schema document's ownership is transferred to the registry. The entry's type is set to external, and its handler and completion-hook fields are cleared.
If no existing entry matches and the registry is at capacity (count equals capacity), the capacity is doubled and the backing array is reallocated to accommodate the new size. The count then increments by one, and the new entry is written at the newly claimed slot.
In both the replacement and growth cases, new copies of the name and path strings are allocated under the registry. Ownership of the schema document is transferred to the registry (not copied).
behavior.adding-an-internal-tool:
Adding an internal tool follows the same replacement and growth logic as adding an external tool, with two differences: - The path field is not set (internal tools have no executable path). - The handler and completion-hook function references are stored in the entry. - The entry type is set to internal.
Internal tool entries have a null path. Code that operates on tool entries must handle a null path field without crashing.
behavior.clearing: Clearing the registry frees the name string, path string (if non-null), and schema document of every entry. The entry count is reset to zero. The backing array capacity is preserved — subsequent additions reuse the existing allocation.
behavior.sorting: If the registry has one or fewer entries, sorting is a no-op. Otherwise, all entries are sorted in-place in ascending alphabetical order by name (lexicographic, byte-value comparison). This is the order in which entries are enumerated and in which they appear in the JSON tools array.
behavior.building-the-llm-tools-array: A JSON array is produced containing a deep copy of every tool's schema root object, in the order the entries appear in the registry (typically alphabetical after sorting). The resulting array is a mutable JSON structure suitable for inclusion in an LLM request.
